steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.quaerere: 'Quaerere' is a present active infinitive expressing purpose
hints.facere: 'Facere' is a present active infinitive parallel to 'quaerere'
hints.perierat: 'Perierat' is a finite verb but it is subordinate in the relative clause

hints.present indicative: Present 'venit' looks identical, but in narrative context this is perfect (long -i-)
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'veniet' — 'Venit' is 3rd singular perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'veniebat' — 'Venit' is 3rd singular perfect

hints.vendo, vendere, vendidi, venditum: These are the principal parts of 'vendo' (to sell)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vinco' (to conquer)
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the principal parts of 'video' (to see)

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Filii' — 'Filius' is the nominative subject
hints.vocative: Vocative of 'filius' is 'fili' — 'Filius' here is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'filii' ends in -ii
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'filii' ends in -ii

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'homo' — 'hominis' is the genitive singular

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'hominis' ends in -is

steps.reveal: perierat = had perished / had been lost

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'periit' — 'perierat' with -era- is pluperfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'peribat' — 'perierat' has the pluperfect marker -era-
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'periret' — 'perierat' is indicative with -era-

hints.pario, parere, peperi, partum: These are the principal parts of 'pario' (to give birth/produce)
hints.peto, petere, petivi, petitum: These are the principal parts of 'peto' (to seek/attack)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go) — 'perire' is the compound per + ire

steps.prompt: Why are 'quaerere' and 'facere' in the infinitive?

hints.indirect statement: Indirect statement requires an accusative subject — here the infinitives express purpose after 'venit'
hints.historical infinitive: Historical infinitives replace finite verbs in vivid narrative — that is not the case here
hints.ablative of means: Infinitives do not function as ablatives of means in Latin

hints.causal conjunction (because): 'Quod' can mean 'because' but here it introduces a relative clause referring to 'salvum facere quod perierat'
hints.conjunction (that): 'Quod' can introduce noun clauses, but here it is a relative pronoun with 'perierat'
hints.interrogative adjective: 'Quod' as interrogative means 'which?' — here it is a relative pronoun

translation: For the Son of Man came to seek and to save that which was lost
